issue_title: loading indicator missing for add layer for slow WMSs
issue_body: add new server, then loads GetCaps but doesn't show progress

* **pulsar version**: 2.0.1

## Description

The greenlet pool is used for consuming implicit asynchronous function in a pool of greenlets.
The performance of consuming in the pool is worse than writing explicit asynchronous code.
The reason for the worse performance is the creation of tasks to run in the greenlet pool.

To improve things we should have a task always running which consume tasks from a queue.

## Steps to reproduce
```
python setup.py bench -a green
```
with results
```
TestGreenIo.test_yield_io: repeated 10(x1000) times, average 0.43558 secs, stdev 3.34 %
TestGreenIo.test_green_io: repeated 10(x1000) times, average 0.43577 secs, stdev 3.36 %
TestGreenIo.test_green_pool: repeated 10(x1000) times, average 0.53749 secs, stdev 43.12 %
Ran 3 tests in 5.404s
```

issue_title: Possibility to rename files withing the board
issue_body:
Hello guys! 

Just a quick possible  feature would be to add the option to `rename` files within the board, I don't really know if this is possible but I guess that it would be a kind of merge between:

*  `get` from the board and store it in memory
* `rm` from board.
* `put` the file stored in memory in the new directory.

Maybe something like this in the `cli.py`:
```python
@cli.command()
@click.argument("remote_file")
@click.argument("remote_new_file", type=click.File("wb"), required=True)
def rename(remote_file, remote_new_file):
    # Get the file contents.
    board_files = files.Files(_board)
    contents = board_files.get(remote_file)
    # Delete the provided file/directory on the board.
    board_files = files.Files(_board)
    board_files.rm(remote_file)
    # Put the file on the board.
    board_files.put(remote_filename, contents)
```

I know it involve more than just those couple of line but just wanted to help a bit.

Regards.

issue_title: Cannot import pyassimp on architectures other than x86_64
issue_body:
When attempting to use pyassimp on an i386 architecture it still searches for the assimp library in `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu` causing the following stack traces:

```
>>> import pyassimp
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from .core import *
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/core.py", line 34, in <module>
    class AssimpLib(object):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/core.py", line 38, in AssimpLib
    load, load_mem, export, release, dll = helper.search_library()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/helper.py", line 233, in search_library
    raise AssimpError("assimp library not found")
pyassimp.errors.AssimpError: assimp library not found
>>> import pyassimp
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from .core import *
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyassimp/core.py", line 28, in <module>
    from . import structs
ImportError: cannot import name structs
```

With the second stack trace repeated for further attempts.

The x86_64 library path is hard coded into the list of directories to search, see [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/port/PyAssimp/pyassimp/helper.py#L30). Perhaps [search_library](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/port/PyAssimp/pyassimp/helper.py#L198) should be replaced with [find_library](https://docs.python.org/2/library/ctypes.html#finding-shared-libraries) from ctypes.

issue_title: Add some tests on the files exported by the datastore
issue_body: In particular the zip files must contain flat names, not a tree.

They are a source of very hard to debug issues. In particular the fact that the `.filter` method can return sometimes a FilteredSiteCollection and sometime a SiteCollection, Also, they have different `_slots_` and there are properties attached after class creation which are really ugly.

issue_title: LegacyConnection fails to import with Django 1.4
issue_body: Django 1.4 appears to have moved or removed LegacyConnection, so it fails to import after upgrading.

Billiard is used in celery which officially supports python 3.3, therefore billiard must support python 3.3 as well. It looks like it often has issues after each update related to python 3.3 compatibility and it will help to detect that automatically.

```
                service_name, 'resources-1')
            raise UnknownAPIVersionError(
                service_name, api_version, ', '.join(available_api_versions))
```

issue_title: range() integer end argument expected, got str

* https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/d4094511f7c5f084133578bb4d5a30befa7ccbe8/botocore/credentials.py#L47
* https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/42b49b103662048fed22fc9debfbde78e11d5686/botocore/utils.py#L140

This is an easy fix, just apply `int()` to the values that must be integers - `metadata_service_num_attempts` is only one of them.

```
[default]
metadata_service_num_attempts = 10

awscli==1.6.5
botocore==0.76.0
```

ISO 8601 allows for a special case of midnight: `24:00:00`.

For example `2007-04-05T24:00` is the same instant as `2007-04-06T00:00`

But ciso8601 currently supports the latter:

```
In [1]: import ciso8601

In [2]: ciso8601.parse_datetime('2007-04-06T00:00')
Out[2]: datetime.datetime(2007, 4, 6, 0, 0)

In [3]: ciso8601.parse_datetime('2007-04-05T24:00')
Out[3]: None
```

Tested on ciso8601 version 1.0.7

```
--- /dev/null
+++ a/test/util.py
@@ -0,0 +1,18 @@
+from click.testing import CliRunner
+from ykman.cli.__main__ import cli
+import os
+
+
+PKG_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
+
+
+def open_file(*relative_path):
+    return open(os.path.join(PKG_DIR, 'files', *relative_path), 'rb')
+
+
+def ykman_cli(*argv, **kwargs):
+    runner = CliRunner()
+    result = runner.invoke(cli, list(argv), obj={}, **kwargs)
+    if result.exit_code != 0:
+        raise result.exception
+    return result.output
```

issue_title: Single value indexing of DataArray returns 0d array
issue_body:
When you access a single element from a DataArray, you get a 0d array. These are generally hard to work with since they don't behave like normal arrays or scalars.

In addition, this breaks the current implementation of `apply_polynomial` in pycore since `np.polyval` relies on iteration over the supplied array.

OBSERVE:
```python
import nixio as nix


nixfile = nix.File.open("nixfile.nix", mode=nix.FileMode.Overwrite,
                        backend="h5py")
blk = nixfile.create_block("blk", "blk")
dataarray = blk.create_data_array("da", "da", data=[1.1, 2.5, 3.42])

# accessing single value returns 0d array
print("Shape {}".format(dataarray[1].shape))
print("Ndim  {}".format(dataarray[1].ndim))

for v in dataarray:
    print("Shape {}".format(v.shape))
    print("Ndim  {}".format(v.ndim))

# these can be hard to work with if you're writing code that generally works
# with arrays
val = dataarray[2]
try:
    val[:]
except IndexError as exc:
    print("IndexError caught: {}".format(exc))

try:
    for v in val:
        print(v)
except TypeError as exc:
    print("TypeError caught: {}".format(exc))

# currently, it also breaks polynomial coeffients
dataarray.polynom_coefficients = (0, 1.0)

print(dataarray[0])
```

Three options:
1. Keep the return value as is and fix `apply_polynomial`.
2. Return a normal numpy array with shape `(1,)`.
3. Return a numpy scalar or python value.

1 & 3 don't solve the issue of iterating over the value returned by indexing an array, but that might be OK. 3 in particular is probably the most pythonic since it mimics indexing lists or any other iterable. 2 is probably a _general_ solution, but it's not nice since it doesn't behave the way iterables tend to work (not even numpy arrays).

I'd love to hear thoughts.

issue_title: virtualenv hangs while creating env near big .html files
issue_body:
I've stumbled upon the fact that ``virtualenv`` just hang when I was creating an environment in my ``~/tmp`` dir. Looking at ``top`` output I found out that ``pip`` process (or more precisely ``python -c 'import sys, pip; sys.exit(pip.main....``) was eating ~100% of CPU and a lot of memory (GBs).

Digging into the issue I found out that pip was parsing huge (if you call 64 Mb huge) ``.html`` file I had in ``~/tmp`` and I think was trying to determine its encoding. That process took a lot of CPU time which looked like it just hang.

I wondered why would pip parse arbitrary ``.html`` files in current directory. After stepping out of ~~rabbit hole~~ stack, I found out that for some reason ``virtualenv`` passes current dir in ``find_links`` to ``pip``.

It looks like this ``'.'`` has been in code since forever, but I can't find (didn't dig deep enough) the reason for it. We don't want to install stuff from current dir, do we? I think we should remove everything except ``virtualenv_support`` dir from ``file_search_dirs`` method since everything we want to install is in that dir anyway.

issue_title: Enable more consistent activation when using virtualenv's python binary directly
issue_body:
As mentioned in #1080 the current documentation, to me, implies that using an activation script and then executing python functions the same as running `/<virtualenv>/bin/python` directly--which is not the case because the latter does not modify any environment variables:

> If you directly run a script or the python interpreter from the virtualenv's bin/ directory (e.g. path/to/ENV/bin/pip or /path/to/ENV/bin/python-script.py) there's no need for activation.

(And, my experience with virtualenv and Ansible inventory scripts described in #1080 gave an example of where this difference in behavior caused hard-to-debug issues.)

It was stated in https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/906#issuecomment-244394963 that this behavior is intentional:

> the Python executable doesn't set the PATH or VIRTUAL_ENV environment variables. That's by design, and is not a bug.

The underlying reason given for this intentional behavior is:

>  I don;'t want python in a virtualenv to work differently than the system python.

This intrigued me because I'd assumed that virtualenv already worked differently to system Python in order implement its functionality, so now I wondered how the functionality of changing the `sys.path` was implemented.

AFAICT this functionality is implemented within <https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/blob/master/virtualenv_embedded/site.py>. And from this I learned about the potential for a `sitecustomize` module (e.g. `site-packages/sitecustomize.py`) as described in <https://docs.python.org/2/library/site.html>.

And, while a process can't modify its parent process's environment variables, it *can* modify its own environment variables and these will be inherited by any subprocesses.

With this discovery I created `/<virtualenv path>/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sitecustomize.py` based on `activate_this.py`:

```python
import os
import sys 

old_os_path = os.environ.get('PATH', '')
os.environ['PATH'] = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(sys.executable)) + os.pathsep + old_os_path
```

(Clearly this is just a quick proof-of-concept, to be robust the code would need to avoid duplication of paths and also modify the other environment variables.)

And with this file in place I was able to run the original command (that uses the Amazon EC2 inventory script as per <http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/intro_dynamic_inventory.html#example-aws-ec2-external-inventory-script>) successfully:

       ansible -i ec2.py --list-hosts all

Note: 

 * In my case Ansible had been installed via a recent version of Homebrew.
 * These are relevant details about the installation:

       $ which ansible
       /usr/local/bin/ansible
       
       $ head -1 `which ansible`
       #!/usr/local/Cellar/ansible/2.3.2.0_1/libexec/bin/python
 * It's possible to test this functionality without a Amazon EC2 account--you'll just get a `boto.exception.NoAuthHandlerFound: No handler was ready to authenticate` error instead of the expected list of hosts.

This functionality is definitely useful for me (in terms of creating a more "isolated 'virtual' python environment" for Python-based applications as Homebrew uses it) and given the comments on #906 it seems to have potential for others as well.

Have I missed any unintended side-effects from using this approach? Is using the `sitecustomize.py` approach consistent with the desire to remain consistent with the system Python functionality?

Even if deemed not consistent enough to include in virtualenv it seems documenting this approach would be helpful for those who wish to implement it themselves.

Thanks for considering this proposal.

Procedure to build and upload docs to https://pythonhosted.org/cssselect/ used to be:

```
(pip install sphinx)
python setup.py build_sphinx
python setup.py upload_sphinx
```

But now you get:

```
$ python setup.py upload_sphinx
running upload_sphinx
Submitting documentation to https://upload.pypi.org/legacy/
Upload failed (410): Uploading documentation is no longer supported, we recommend using https://readthedocs.org/.
```

For 1.0.0, I had to manually create the zip file from the docs _build/html folder and upload it with PyPI's web interface.

### Problem

The CSS3 spec allows the namespace field to be left empty, which indicates
an element with *no* namespace attached. However, cssselect cannot handle
those selectors right now. 

For example, suppose we have the following line:

```python
GenericTranslator().css_to_xpath('|foo')
```

This causes the parser to raise an exception:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/xpath.py", line 192, in css_to_xpath
    for selector in parse(css))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 354, in parse
    return list(parse_selector_group(stream))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 367, in parse_selector_group
    yield Selector(*parse_selector(stream))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 375, in parse_selector
    result, pseudo_element = parse_simple_selector(stream)
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 475, in parse_simple_selector
    "Expected selector, got %s" % (peek,))
cssselect.parser.SelectorSyntaxError: Expected selector, got <DELIM '|' at 0>
```

### Expected behaviour

cssselect should be able to handle a selector like `|foo`.

### Note

Here is the related part from [Selector Level 3 $6.1.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/selectors-3/#typenmsp):

```
ns|E
    elements with name E in namespace ns 
*|E
    elements with name E in any namespace, including those without a namespace 
|E
    elements with name E without a namespace 
E
    if no default namespace has been declared for selectors, this is equivalent to *|E.
    Otherwise it is equivalent to ns|E where ns is the default namespace. 
```

issue_title: Spout prematurely acks, even failed Bolt tuples
issue_body:
Hello,

I've had problems calling a Spouts `fail()` method in the boilerplate wordcount project. According to the pystorm [quickstart docs](https://github.com/pystorm/pystorm/blob/master/doc/source/quickstart.rst#failed-tuples) and numerous things I've read, calling `fail(tuple)` in a Bolt should elicit a failure in the originating Spout. However, even with the few modifications I've made, I always get a Spout `ack()` right when it leaves the Spout. Is this the correct behavior, or do I need to change a setting/instance variable?

I'm on `streamparse 3.4.0` and `storm 1.0.2`. 

My logs show the Spout `ack()` coming before the Bolt logging.

```
7609 [Thread-14-word_spout-executor[2 2]] INFO  o.a.s.s.ShellSpout - ShellLog pid:35761, name:word_spout 2017-02-20 15:30:33,070 - pystorm.component.word_spout - acking w
hen I shouldnt tup_id: 3219

...

7611 [Thread-21] INFO  o.a.s.t.ShellBolt - ShellLog pid:35760, name:count_bolt 2017-02-20 15:30:33,072 - pystorm.component.count_bolt - BOLT: receiving tup_id/count: 3219

```

`word.py`
```
from itertools import cycle

from streamparse import Spout
from streamparse import ReliableSpout


class WordSpout(ReliableSpout):
    outputs = ['word']

    def initialize(self, stormconf, context):
        self.words = cycle(['dog', 'cat', 'zebra', 'elephant'])
        self.count = 0

    def next_tuple(self):
        self.count += 1
        word = next(self.words)
        self.emit([str(self.count)], tup_id=self.count)

    def ack(self, tup_id):
        self.logger.info("acking when I shouldnt tup_id: {0}".format(tup_id))

    def fail(self, tup_id):
        self.logger.info("failing when I should tup_id: {0}".format(tup_id))

```

`wordcount.py`

```
import os
from collections import Counter

from streamparse import Bolt


class WordCountBolt(Bolt):
    auto_ack = False
    auto_fail = False

    outputs = ['word', 'count']

    def initialize(self, conf, ctx):
        self.counter = Counter()
        self.pid = os.getpid()
        self.total = 0

    def _increment(self, word, inc_by):
        self.counter[word] += inc_by
        self.total += inc_by

    def process(self, tup):
        word = tup.values[0]
        self._increment(word, 10 if word == "dog" else 1)
        # if self.total % 1000 == 0:
            # self.logger.info("counted [{:,}] words [pid={}]".format(self.total,
                                                                    # self.pid))
        self.logger.info("BOLT: receiving tup_id/count: {0}".format(word))
        # self.emit([word, self.counter[word]])
        self.fail(tup)
```
To reiterate, from what I know, I would expect the Bolt's failing to elicit a fail in the Spout and I would also not expect an ack so early. Am I wrong in thinking?

-jgujgu

issue_title: Not all imported utility functions are described in the docs
issue_body:
https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/1151b511a10ec2568d72b94469afb09040a5ed9d/skimage/__init__.py#L46

Proposed fix:
  1. Only import the utilities functions that we describe.
  2. Describe all utility functions that are actually imported.

To do 1. you would need to specify the functions we import in the line
```python
from .util.dtypes import *
```

To do 2. you would need to look at the file `skimage.util.dtypes` at the line
```python
__all__ = [ ... ]
```
and give a short description of the functions that are imported.



Python lesson: the list [`__all__`](https://docs.python.org/3.6/tutorial/modules.html#importing-from-a-package) describes the functions that are imported when you use `from XXXX import *`

issue_title: BATCH operations could support authentication in the headers
issue_body:
Currently, BATCH operations need each request to specify their authentication. This is okay for BasicAuth but can be trickier for other authentication schemes. For instance, hawk needs each request to be signed with the content of the request, which adds complexity on the client side for almost no benefit.

One way to solve this and make it easier for clients would be to allow the specification of credentials in the BATCH request headers, and do not need to specify it during the sub-requests.

issue_title: Update bitfield definitions to be defined via yaml

issue_body:
The change to using UUID type and the following upgrade script introduced in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/commit/e8f6551d9787ceec99248b2b0ad04b8f22a0d8b4 causes a problem with truncated values for the existing values - essentially corrupting the data (observed locally):
```
>>> Applying workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns (Resize uuid columns on workflows tables.)
>>> workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns: WARNING: Data truncated for column 'uuid' at row 1
>>> workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns: WARNING: Data truncated for column 'uuid' at row 2
...
```
Before the uuid column was `db.String(36)` and a value included hyphens `00011d78-fd76-11e4-b9a6-ecf4bb24`. With the UUID type on MySQL, the hyphens are not considered and the length of the CHAR type is set to 32. During the upgrade the data is still stored with hyphens causing the truncation. 

I noticed this in the output of the upgrade, the corrupt IDs then cause a range of [troubles](https://gist.github.com/anonymous/53c521015b0c34685173) post-upgrade.

I suggest we either (a) change the MySQL type to `CHAR(36)`, or (b) remove hyphens from the data during upgrade.

@hachreak @jirikuncar

There was no warning and there was no way to catch it til after the fact that it had happened. (it wouldn't been caught if we wouldn't build with FORTIFY_SOURCE).

I would recommend that you flip the default setting around and by default build with `BROTLI_BUILD_PORTABLE` or change it to `BROTLI_BUILD_NONPORTABLE`, because the memcpy implementation is outside of your control, you can not verify or warn if things gets miscompiled and relying on "undefined behavior" is generally a bad idea.

issue_title: Inaccurate conversion from kilometers to miles
issue_body:
geopy/units.py uses the exact multiplier, 1.609344, to convert miles
into kilometers but an *approximate* multiplier, 0.621371192, to convert
kilometers into miles.  This leads to an error of about 4mm over a
quarter meridian.  Not much, you might say.  But why be so sloppy in the
first place?  Either express the multiplier with 17 significant figures
(0.62137119223733397) or divide by 1.609344.

issue_title: Allow to specify custom Agent for Twisted transport
issue_body:
Currently Twisted transport uses default t.w.c.Agent class that is limited to basic BrowserLikePolicyForHTTPS that does not support client-side SSL certificate validation. This is normally changed by specifying contextFactory argument to Agent constructor. I might be able to overcome this limitation by replacing transport for twisted+https scheme by my own class but I guess no documentation on this feature means this API is not public.

This is specific use-case for on-premise instances behind proxies.

issue_title: Issue with classes inheriting from Resource
issue_body:
When creating a class that inherits from Resource, any resource generated from inside the subclass is initialized as the subclass (not as Resource).

Exemple:

```
from rdflib import *
from rdflib.resource import Resource
from rdflib.namespace import RDF, FOAF

class Person( Resource ):

	def __init__(self, graph, subject):
		super(Person, self).__init__(graph, subject)
		self.add( RDF.type, FOAF.Person )

	def interest(self):
		return self.value( FOAF.interest ) # that's the point
		
g = Graph()
mike = Person( g, URIRef('http://example.com/mike' ) )
any = Resource( g, URIRef('http://example.com/any' ) )
mike.add( FOAF.interest, any)
interest = mike.interest() 
print g.serialize(format='turtle')
```
Outputs:
```
...
@prefix ns1: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .
<http://example.com/mike> a ns1:Person ;
    ns1:interest <http://example.com/any> .
<http://example.com/any> a ns1:Person .     <---- Who said that!?
```

Edit: rdflib version is 4.2.1

Cheers,
Marc

issue_title: Jupyter notebook "hangs" when trying to display a Frame
issue_body: The issue stems from the console's "interactive" mode of display: when a table is shown in a console, it allows the user to scroll by listening to key presses. However in Jupyter notebook keypresses are not delivered to the terminal, and therefore the frame gets "stuck" (cannot exit interactive mode unless q is pressed).

issue_title: Change icon "ban-circle" to conform to international sign-design guidelines
issue_body:
The Ban-Sign in Font-Awesome features a Circle, striked through by a bar from left bottom to right top. That is not a proper ban sign, just look at international traffic-signs. There a ban is always the reverse sign of yours, while your's means releasing some ban. 

Ban sign always has a bar going from left top to right bottom. The graphic-designers explanation for that sign: 
In psychology the direction of "future" is to the right, coming from left. Also positive is to the top, while negative is to the bottom. So a sign showing a strike-through like the ban-sign of Font-Awesome is actually releasing a ban, not setting it, because the bar goes up and shows a move to the positive. That's why a proper ban-sign has a bar coming from the top going down to the negative bottom symbolizing a ban. No = down! 

Please either change the icon's strike-through bar direction or do another ban-icon which is designed the other way round. Thanks! ;-) 

I've attached an image of a proper ban-sign: 
![10236730 height 150 width 150 no-ban-sign](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/915591/908825/88a57df6-fda8-11e2-80fd-ee2520dd8e7a.png)

BTW: Yes, there are many signs like your's out there, but you know: Somebody sometimes got it wrong and it spread around and now most of the people don't know what the correct sign is. But that doesn't mean that the reversed signs are correct. ;-)

issue_title: Erroneous warnings produced since #500 (Function argument not defined in specification)
issue_body:
### Description
It looks like #500 introduced a bug which reports that the function arguments weren't defined when they in fact were.

### Expected behaviour
Function arguments with proper definitions should not produce warnings.


### Actual behaviour
Function arguments with proper definitions do produce warnings.


### Steps to reproduce
Consider the following swagger spec:
```yaml
swagger: "2.0"
basePath: "/v1"
schemes:
- "https"
paths:
  /photo:
    post:
      summary: "Upload a photo along with its metadata"
      description: ""
      operationId: "upload.upload_photo"
      consumes:
      - "multipart/form-data"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: "photo"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "file"
        description: "The photo file"
      - name: "imageId"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "string"
        description: "The UUID assigned to this photo"
      - name: "associations"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "array"
        collectionFormat: "csv"
        items:
          type: "string"
        description: "A CSV list of QR IDs associated with the photo"
      - name: "takenAt"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "string"
```

issue_title: Travis deploy tagged releases to PyPI
issue_body: I'm creating this issue partly to have a place to discuss this.

Currently, [section 4.f](http://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#4.f) of the OpenType Feature File Syntax specification says:

> NOTE! If a GDEF table is not explicitly defined in the feature file, then an implementation of this syntax will create one. In this case, it will use the set of defined mark classes to define the mark glyphs for the GDEF GlyphClass. In this case, all mark glyph classes must be disjoint, and no base glyph may be used also as a mark glyph.

However, makeotf accepts the following feature file:
```
markClass [acute] <anchor 350 0> @MarksA;
markClass [acute] <anchor 350 0> @MarksB;
```

and makes this output for it:
```
  <GDEF>
    <Version value="1.0"/>
    <GlyphClassDef>
      <ClassDef glyph="acute" class="3"/>
    </GlyphClassDef>
  </GDEF>
```

Should “all mark glyph classes must be disjoint” be removed from the above paragraph?

issue_title: BigQuery `insert_data()` fails with record type columns (nested objects)
issue_body:
BigQuery [tabledata.insertAll](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/tabledata/insertAll) supports inserting rows with columns that take record types (nested objects).

A customer reports that

> My table structure has nested schemas. The [[google-cloud-python] docs](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/bigquery-table.html#google.cloud.bigquery.table.Table.insert_data) don't say how to handle nested structures. All we get is, "rows (list of tuples) – Row data to be inserted. Each tuple should contain data for each schema field on the current table and in the same order as the schema fields." A list of tuples. That's it. If I have nested data do I make sub-tuples? Is it simply not supported at this time? 
> 
> I dug into the BQ code to find out what was going on. BQ does, 
> 
> zip(table._schema, rows) 
> 
> which looks like it won't support nested inserts (or could -- with sub-tuples, but who knows). 
> 

Example in the API explorer, with the following table schema:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/247555/22127258/1743ad4a-de50-11e6-9a71-94433ecded92.png)

Try it out in the [API Explorer for bigquery.tabledata.insertAll](https://developers.google.com/apis-explorer/#p/bigquery/v2/bigquery.tabledata.insertAll). Enter the following for the request body (after creating a table with the same schema). You need to switch the editor to free-form mode

```
{
  "rows": 
  [
    {
      "insert_id": "12345",
      "json": 
      {
        "string_col": "Some value",
        "record_col": {
          "nested_string": "another string value",
          "nested_repeated": ["0", "1", "2"],
          "nested_record": {
            "nested_nested_string": "some deep insight"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

I can verify that this works with a SQL query.

```
#standardSql
SELECT * FROM `swast-scratch.hello_world.nested` LIMIT 1000
```

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/247555/22130561/a792fdb8-de61-11e6-8f5a-f25f52dfdc31.png)

It is not clear from the docs how to do a similar call from our client libraries (and it likely isn't possible).

issue_title: Android examples cannot all be compiled in one run
issue_body:
Before isolated compile, the `unpacked_libraries` and `dx_compile` tasks operated over AndroidLibrary targets. But now they need to be refactored to work over AndroidBinary targets instead. 

Those tasks unpack .aar archives and create AndroidLibrary targets from their contents. This included adding a JarLibrary dependency (from the classes.jar inside the aar archives) to every dependent AndroidLibrary. But those classes.jars are now needed on the classpath of the actual AndroidBinary targets in order to compile. This previously just worked under global_compile strategy, but in a correctness sense those jars should be dependencies of the AndroidBinaries.

I have hacked the tests to go green in an attempt to move android backend to contrib. But if a pants run globs multiple android targets, the graph gets constructed incorrectly and dependencies are missing.

To repro: pants bundle examples/src/android::
To fix: have unpacked_libraries operate on AndroidBinary targets. That change is not so bad but it will also have to be reflected in DxCompile, which filters each unpacked_library based on the include/exclude pattern of the Binary's AndroidLibrary targets. So a major rethink is possibly in order.

issue_title: Create a more robust product for ivy_imports
issue_body:
As discussed in [RB 2467](https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2467/diff/1/?file=2206672#file2206672line306), the product for ivy_imports is only a 
ProductMapping of ```{ builddir: [jar_filenames]}```.

Some tasks, including the proposed UnpackLibraries for the Android 
backend, could use more data about the jars themselves. Currently that
task has had to resort to parsing fields from the name created by ivy.

In this proposed spec, the product would be a list of ImportedJars, where an 
ImportedJar has a slot for the path and a slot for the metadata, i.e. org, name, 
version, etc. See the [IvyModuleRef](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/ivy_utils.py#L38-38)

A TODO is added to ivy_imports.py in RB 2467 and when that lands, 
this issue will track the changes to the product.

issue_title: Cache artifacts may be partially extracted
issue_body:
The LocalArtifactCache `store_and_use_artifact` method streams an artifact to a temporary location, renames it to its final location, and then attempts to extract it before returning successfully.

This does not account for the case of corrupted tarballs, where a partially extracted result may be left behind, and which (in the case of incremental builds) can result in non-determinism.

Luckily, the fix looks relatively straightforward in the case of incremental builds: removing the output dir if extraction fails.

A few ideas stumbled upon while trying to speed up CI burns on #6104:
+ Cache src/rust/engine/target in .travis.yml
   The official rust language support in Travis caches `~/.cargo` and `target/`, so this is kosher from their point of view and blessed by Chrichton. I tried this already and the upshot is all 3rdparty crate compilations are cached when they have not changed versions - easy win.
+ Throttle `cargo build` as we do for zinc in pants.travis-ci.ini
   This can be done via `CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=N` in .travis.yml and might give us better throughput with less thrash. Measurements needed.
+ Throttle `cargo test` similar to above.
   Not sure how to best do this. There is rumor `RUST_TEST_THREADS=N` works, though it looks discouraged. I've had great luck with [`cargo make ...`](https://crates.io/crates/cargo-make) which allows setting up arbitrary commands in a `Makefile.toml` on a side project. Might be a way to go for these bottom two bullets. Likely very little bang for the buck here though, tests are still pretty fast.

/cc @stuhood 
When I was having a look at the Scala Zinc integration, I've noticed that you don't use the `ClassLoaderCache` when instantiating Scala's incremental compiler:
```scala
  def newScalaCompiler(instance: XScalaInstance, interfaceJar: File): AnalyzingCompiler =
    new AnalyzingCompiler(
      instance,
      CompilerBridgeProvider.constant(interfaceJar, instance),
      ClasspathOptionsUtil.auto,
      _ => (), None
    )
```
instead of what our default Zinc utils do:  
```scala
  def scalaCompiler(
      scalaInstance: xsbti.compile.ScalaInstance,
      compilerBridgeJar: File,
      classpathOptions: ClasspathOptions
  ): AnalyzingCompiler = {
    val bridgeProvider = constantBridgeProvider(scalaInstance, compilerBridgeJar)
    val emptyHandler = (_: Seq[String]) => ()
    val loader = Some(new ClassLoaderCache(new URLClassLoader(Array())))
    new AnalyzingCompiler(
      scalaInstance,
      bridgeProvider,
      classpathOptions,
      emptyHandler,
      loader
    )
  }
```
  
Note the use of `Some` instead of `None`.
  
Using the classloader cache can provide great speedups, as evidenced by https://github.com/sbt/sbt/pull/2754.
  
Is this an oversight or have you tried this option in the past and didn't happen to improve performance for Pants?

issue_title: Call topsort at start of `simple_codegen_task` execution.
issue_body:
Cycles in the BUILD graph can case infinite recursion with very unhelpful error messages for developers. They trigger at the very beginning of `execute()`, when pants tries to compute the transitive invalidation hash for a target with cycles:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/codegen/tasks/simple_codegen_task.py#L177

We need to make sure topsort's been called before doing that.

Maybe we should make a `topsort` product that simple codegen task requires? And a task that just does the topsort if topsort hasn't already happened.

Good day,

I have a suspicion that time() decorator is not thread-safe in some way. Consider the following code:

```python
from prometheus_client import start_http_server, Summary
import sys
import time
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from timeit import default_timer

S_TIME = Summary("timing_with_time", "Time spent processing request")
S_MANUAL = Summary("timing_manually", "Time spent processing request")

@S_TIME.time()
def process_request():
    """A dummy function that takes some time."""
    start = default_timer()
    time.sleep(1)
    S_MANUAL.observe(max(default_timer() - start, 0))
    
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    start_http_server(8000)

    threads = int(sys.argv[1])
        
    pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=threads)
    for i in range(1000):
        pool.submit(process_request)
```

Running it as `python bug.py 1` produce the following metrics:
```
timing_with_time_count 12.0
timing_with_time_sum 12.013219268759713
timing_manually_count 12.0
timing_manually_sum 12.012461822945625
```
I.e. both _sum values are similar as expected. However if I increase the concurrency, then the metric that is
tracked with `time()` does NOT have its _sum increasing:

With `python bug.py 2`:
```
timing_with_time_count 10.0
timing_with_time_sum 5.004416585084982
timing_manually_count 10.0
timing_manually_sum 10.009211090975441
```
I.e. the count matches, but the sum of the first one is wrong! I did 10 requests each 1 second long, not 0.5 second long. The problem scales linearly. E.g. with `python bug.py 10`:
```
timing_with_time_count 60.0
timing_with_time_sum 6.0030247694812715
timing_manually_count 60.0
timing_manually_sum 60.06247438094579
```
I.e. _sum metric measured with `time()` decorator always thinks it's single threaded.

issue_title: Person model returns a CorporationName label
issue_body:
In `probablepeople/__init__.py` line 84, this string 'M.A. HSG in Law Seraina Williams' is returning 'CorporationName' label even though the type is set to 'person'. 

raw_string : 'M.A. HSG in Law Seraina Williams'
tokens : ['M.A.', 'HSG', 'in', 'Law', 'Seraina', 'Williams']
tags : ['GivenName', 'CorporationName', 'CorporationName', 'CorporationName', 'CorporationName', 'Surname']

This issue is breaking the dedupe workflow in parseratorvariable (https://github.com/dedupeio/parseratorvariable).
Since I'm using the `Person Name` FieldType, this issue is raised : 
```
Exception in thread Thread-6:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.4_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/threading.py", line 916, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.4_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/threading.py", line 864, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "/Users/aspirant/Documents/bitbucket/projects/person-dedupe/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/dedupe/core.py", line 76, in __call__
    filtered_pairs = self.fieldDistance(record_pairs)
  File "/Users/aspirant/Documents/bitbucket/projects/person-dedupe/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/dedupe/core.py", line 101, in fieldDistance
    distances = self.data_model.distances(records)
  File "/Users/aspirant/Documents/bitbucket/projects/person-dedupe/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/dedupe/datamodel.py", line 82, in distances
    record_2[field])
  File "/Users/aspirant/Documents/bitbucket/projects/person-dedupe/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/parseratorvariable/__init__.py", line 90, in comparator
    variable_type = self.variable_types[variable_type_1]
KeyError: 'Corporation'
```

Either parseratorvariable is not handling the case of probablepeople is returning a wrong label or probablepeople is not returning an error if the label doesn't correspond to the type 'person'.

For those who wants to patch this with a work around, what I have done is replacing the `comparator` method in `parseratorvariable/__init__` (line 54) : 
Add this lines where you are using dedupe library
```
import dedupe
import numpy
import parseratorvariable
from probableparsing import RepeatedLabelError

def comparator(self, field_1, field_2):
    distances = numpy.zeros(self.expanded_size)
    i = 0

    if not (field_1 and field_2):
        return distances

    distances[i] = 1
    i += 1

    try:
        parsed_variable_1, variable_type_1 = self.tagger(field_1)
        parsed_variable_2, variable_type_2 = self.tagger(field_2)
    except RepeatedLabelError as e:
        if self.log_file:
            import csv
            with open(self.log_file, 'a') as f:
                writer = csv.writer(f)
                writer.writerow([e.original_string.encode('utf8')])
        distances[i:3] = [1, 0]
        distances[-1] = self.compareString(field_1, field_2)
        return distances

    if 'Ambiguous' in (variable_type_1, variable_type_2):
        distances[i:3] = [1, 0]
        distances[-1] = self.compareString(field_1, field_2)
        return distances
    elif variable_type_1 != variable_type_2:
        distances[i:3] = [0, 0]
        distances[-1] = self.compareString(field_1, field_2)
        return distances
    elif variable_type_1 == variable_type_2:
        distances[i:3] = [0, 1]

    if variable_type_1 not in self.variable_types:
        distances[i:3] = [1, 0]
        distances[-1] = self.compareString(field_1, field_2)
        return distances
    
    i += 2

    variable_type = self.variable_types[variable_type_1]

    distances[i:i + self.n_type_indicators] = variable_type['indicator']
    i += self.n_type_indicators

    i += variable_type['offset']
    for j, dist in enumerate(variable_type['compare'](parsed_variable_1,
                                                      parsed_variable_2),
                             i):
        distances[j] = dist

    unobserved_parts = numpy.isnan(distances[i:j + 1])
    distances[i:j + 1][unobserved_parts] = 0
    unobserved_parts = (~unobserved_parts).astype(int)
    distances[(i + self.n_parts):(j + 1 + self.n_parts)] = unobserved_parts

    return distances

parseratorvariable.ParseratorType.comparator = comparator
```
Then you can use dedupe.

When importing a channel, refresh the page and then cancel the import. Then without refreshing the page again, delete the channel. Since the channel content doesn't exist, it will show an error and make the server crash. However, the logging looks fine as there were no error messages. I can only restart the server by deleting the `.kolibri` folder first.

## System information

 - Version: 0.6
 - Operating system: Mac OS
 - Browser: Chrome


## How to reproduce

1. Import a channel
2. Refresh the page
3. Cancel the import
4. Delete the channel

## Screenshots

![kolibrierror](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19424916/31193472-0a874c82-a8f9-11e7-9437-6112b0f250b3.gif)

issue_title: sqlalchemy MATCH support is not properly escaped
issue_body:
Adding a single quote to a query utilizing MATCH will break the query. I modified the test case to induce the behavior:

```
    def test_simple_match(self):
        query = self.session.query(self.Character.name) \
                    .filter(match(self.Character.name, 'Trill\'ian'))
        self.assertSQL(
            "SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill\\'ian')",
            query
        )
```

Result:

```
Failure
Expected :"SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill\\'ian')"
Actual   :"SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill'ian')"
```

The output of the test error is a little misleading - the comparison is of course taking place without the escapes.

The API call ``upload_sample`` is broken In PyMISP 2.4.81 with MISP 2.4.81.

The following code is expected to add a sample to an existing event:

```
import pymisp

url = "https://..."
key = "..." 
misp = pymisp.PyMISP(url, key)

event = misp.new_event(info="Upload Sample does not write into this event") 
eventid = event['Event']['id']
with open('test.txt', 'w') as w:
    w.write('test file')
misp.upload_sample("test", "test.txt", eventid)
```

Instead of adding the sample to the existing event, a second event is created with title "Malware samples uploaded on 2017-10-23".

Is there any interest in adding Bootstrap 4-compatible boilerplate templates? I've been modifying the templates to be more Bootstrap 4-friendly while staying close to the Bootstrap 3 templates. Here's a preview:

![screenshot from 2018-07-27 22-54-22](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1728528/43351913-3e0a8174-91f0-11e8-923b-3b4e11452782.png)

I'd be happy to share them once they're finished. Let me know!

Some details before we start:

- Django App name: sports_demo_odbc
- CLASSPATH is set correctly. Tested using python shell and pyodbc connection.
- Settings
`DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': "django_pyodbc",
        'HOST': "localhost,11001",
        'USER': "akguleri",
        'PASSWORD': "notAdmin",
        'NAME': "sports_demo",
        'OPTIONS': {
           'host_is_server': True,
           'dsn': 'dsn_sports_demo',
           'openedge': True
        },
    }   
} `


I hope you have tried checking if the OpenEdge is supported as the documentation says. I tried this but didn't work. The connection establishment works fine (using _python manage.py runserver_) but the queries made to OpenEdge database are not correct. I fixed most of them by hand and reached to the point where at least the server was started without any errors. 
Below are the errors that I got (in the same order) and fixed them by hand to make it work.

1. Error on fetching TABLES from OpenEdge DB:
Query: 
`File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django_pyodbc-1.1.0-py2.7.egg/django_pyodbc/introspection.py", line 95, in get_table_list
    cursor.execute("SELECT TABLE_NAME, 't' FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_TYPE = 'BASE TABLE'")`
Error: 
`File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django_pyodbc-1.1.0-py2.7.egg/django_pyodbc/base.py", line 495, in execute
    raise utils.DatabaseError(*e.args)
django.db.utils.DatabaseError: ('HY000', '[HY000] [DataDirect][ODBC Progress OpenEdge Wire Protocol driver][OPENEDGE]Table/view/synonynm "INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES" cannot be found. (15814) (-210083) (SQLExecDirectW)')`
Correct Query should be: 
`if <is_opendge>:
       cursor.execute("SELECT TBL, 't' FROM SYSPROGRESS.SYSTABLES WHERE TBLTYPE = 'T'")`

2. SchemaEditorClass attribute:
Error: `File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/db/backends/base/base.py", line 604, in schema_editor
    'The SchemaEditorClass attribute of this database wrapper is still None')
NotImplementedError: The SchemaEditorClass attribute of this database wrapper is still None`
I commented the call to schema_editor (in recorder.py) since it tries to create tables using create_model. There should be some way to control this. I don't want to create/change my OpenEdge schema over this API. Perhaps I'm too new to this area that I don't know how to control this behaviour. It would be nice if you can guide me on this.

3. AttributeError:
Error: `File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django_pyodbc-1.1.0-py2.7.egg/django_pyodbc/compiler.py", line 204, in as_sql
    self._fix_aggregates()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django_pyodbc-1.1.0-py2.7.egg/django_pyodbc/compiler.py", line 180, in _fix_aggregates
    for alias, aggregate in self.query.aggregate_select.items():
AttributeError: 'Query' object has no attribute 'aggregate_select'`
The _fix_aggregates function perhaps is meant to counter some MSSQL behaviour as the comment says in the code. I'm not 100% sure though. Again I used `self.connection.ops.is_openedge` and prevent the execution of that function. 

4. Migration problem again:
Error: `File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/db/backends/utils.py", line 64, in execute  return self.cursor.execute(sql, params)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django_pyodbc-1.1.0-py2.7.egg/django_pyodbc/base.py", line 496, in execute  raise utils.DatabaseError(*e.args)
django.db.utils.DatabaseError: ('HY000', '[HY000] [DataDirect][ODBC Progress OpenEdge Wire Protocol driver][OPENEDGE]Table/view/synonynm "AKGULERI.django_migrations" cannot be found. (15814) (-210083) (SQLExecDirectW)')`
This might be Django framework itself but it tries get something Djnago specific which is not available in OpenEdge DB. Here too I made a dirty fix and avoided execution of such query by replacing it with simple query i.e. `SELECT TOP 1 * FROM SYSPROGRESS.SYSTABLES`. Any guidance on this would be helpful as well.

5. Query quotes: After all this the server started I created a simple model for Customer table (having Meta class db_table = 'PUB.CUSTOMER') of the connected OpenEdge DB. Then using shell I created a instance of Customer and tried to fetch some data using `customer.objects.get(pk=1)`  but was returned the below with an error on the shell. The query it formed was something like this 
`SELECT "PUB.CUSTOMER"."custnum", "PUB.CUSTOMER"."name", "PUB.CUSTOMER"."city" FROM "PUB.CUSTOMER" WHERE "PUB.CUSTOMER"."custnum" = ?`

OpenEdge does not understand queries which have quotes in it. So I change the function quote_name in operations.py to return unquoted name var if self.is_openedge.

Now the query customer.objects.get(pk=1) fetch me results from the DB. But perhaps these fixes should be done properly into the django-pyodbc code.

issue_title: README still specifies that "a sample file will be used" in Docker example
issue_body:
This is no longer the case and causes the error (as follows) when the instructed command is run:

```
ERROR: An index file must be specified if not piping, e.g.,
> ipwb replay /path/to/your/index.cdxj

usage: ipwb replay [-h] [-P [<host:port>]] [index]

Start the ipwb relay system

positional arguments:
  index                 path, URI, or multihash of file to use for replay

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -P [<host:port>], --proxy [<host:port>]
                        Proxy URL
```

Per #196, call to ipwb "config" endpoint is not functioning, which prevents us from obtaining the link to the webui. The latter link may be dynamic (e.g., custom port) based on `~/.ipfs/config`, so a call to obtain this information is necessary over hard-coded assumptions.


This issue was initially reported [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/adwords-api/39kYsLsV3Ys).

There is issue with the BatchJobService in Python 3 where an AttributeError is raised in the **_GetRawOperationsFromXML** method in this line:

```      root = ElementTree.fromstring(raw_request_xml.encode('utf-8'))```

This will work in Python 2 because `str` has an encode method, whereas Python 3's `bytes` does not.

We'll try to sneak this in to an upcoming release and close this issue when it is out.

Can we get support for the following JaaS related CLI calls in libjuju?

## CLI commands
### Activate SLA
`juju sla <essential|standard|advanced> --budget <wallet-name>:<number>`
Essential is the only one supported right now. First time execution will require to accept the SLA, something like this:
`juju agree canonical/sla-terms/1`

### Overview of the wallets
`juju wallets`
Response:
```
Wallet          Monthly Budgeted        Available       Spent
primary*           1000      100            95.41        4.59
Total              1000      100            95.41        4.59
                                                             
Credit limit:      1000
```
### Set budget for wallet
`juju set-wallet <wallet-name> <number>`
Cannot be lower than the already budgeted credits.
Failed response:
> ERROR failed to set the wallet: wallet limit cannot be less than the sum of the budgets`

### Create wallet
`juju create-wallet <wallet-name>`

### Details of a wallet
`juju show-wallet <wallet-name>`
Response:
```
Model                   Spent   Budgeted           By   Usage
sla-prod:admin/cdk1     4.59         100        cmars   4%   
                                                     
Total                   4.59         100                4%   
Wallet                              1000             
Unallocated                          900             
```
### Set budget for current model
`juju budget <number>`
Cannot be lower than credits already spent.
Failed response:
> ERROR failed to update the budget: budget limit below current spend 4.59

Cannot be higher than wallet budget.
Failed response:
> ERROR failed to update the budget: budget limits cannot exceed the wallet limit

### Retrieve metrics
`juju metrics <application-name>`
Response:
```
UNIT        	           TIMESTAMP	    METRIC	VALUE
sojobo-api/0	2017-09-26T21:31:24Z	juju-units	    1
```
There is already a wrapper (not async) available, but not implemented ...
```
  def collect_metrics(self):
        """Collect metrics on this application.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()
```


## API calls
### Rating 
There is no command to retrieve the rating of a user to date containing all the spent credits on all the models for a certain month in a certain year. First time run requires Ubuntu SSO login.
API endpoint:
`https://api.jujucharms.com/omnibus/v3/u/{username}/rating/{yyyy}-{mm}`
Response:
```
{
        models: {
                "7ec613cf-b8f7-48a9-8e6e-055da691cc13": {
                        authorizations: { … }
                        machine-support: {
                                total: {
                                        price: "1.12"
                                }
                                rating: {
                                        price: "0.011"
                                        hours: {
                                                hour-2017_08_03_04: {
                                                        value: "6"
                                                        multiplier: "1"
                                                        total: "6"
                                                }
							… 
                                                hour-2017_08_03_20: {
                                                        value: "6"
                                                        multiplier: "1"
                                                        total: "6"
                                                }
                                        }
                                }
                        }
                        total: {
                                price: "1.12"

…

}
```

issue_title: Add a faq page explaining the  personal data policy of data.gouv.fr
issue_body:
Quelques demandes de retraits de données personnelles dans des fichiers référencés sur data.gouv arrivent parfois sur contact@ plutôt que chez le producteur ou le réutilisateur.

Il serait opportun d'ajouter une page de FAQ pédagogique expliquant que:

1. data.gouv n'est pas l'éditeur de ces sites ou fichiers
2. que les données utilisées par ces sites sont issues de données publiques, avec des textes prévoyant leur publication (parfois même obligatoire)

issue_title: Move the call to `model.cuda()` to before optimizer creation
issue_body:
Because of [this issue](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/2021#issuecomment-325204922).  The optimizer might have state that's initialized from the model parameters, and needs to be on the right device.

This means we should either:
1. Have `cuda_device` be a top-level key in the experiment config, so we can move the model over in `commands.train()` before constructing the optimizer.
2. Move the optimizer creation and the call to `model.cuda()` into `Trainer.from_params()`.

I could go either way.  Calling `model.cuda()` inside of `from_params()` in the second option is a little bit more logic than we like to have in those methods, but not much.  The optimizer conceptually seems like it's part of the trainer, so having the optimizer params inside of the trainer params makes sense.

```
import music21
import copy


class MidiProcessor:

        self.path = midiPath

        midiFile.close()



    def getClipForChord(self, chordName, inversion):
        localScore = copy.deepcopy(self.score)
        for part in localScore.parts:
            flatPart = part.flat
            for element in flatPart.getElementsByClass([music21.note.Note, music21.chord.Chord]):
                element.volume.velocity = 63
                element.volume.velocityIsRelative = False
            measureOffset = part[0].timeSignature.numerator
            for chord in flatPart.getElementsByClass(music21.chord.Chord):

                    offset = chord.offset
                    measureIndex = int(offset // measureOffset)
                    clip = localScore.measures(measureIndex - 1, measureIndex + 1)
                    clip[0].insert(0, self.tempo)
                    self.writeStreamToPath(clip, "1.mid")
```
the `getClipForChord()` should locate specific chords in a midifile and change the velocity of these chords to emphasis them for playback (I first initialize the velocity of all the elements to 63). I just get started using music21 and this might be a silly problem, I'm sorry for that. Any help would be appreciated.

issue_title: DOC/API: Add all kwargs to user-facing linking functions?
issue_body:
Since the refactoring of the linking code for `find_link`, the various linking functions have mostly relied on `**kwargs`, and those options are only fully documented for `link()`; the other docstrings refer the user to the `link()` documentation.

This is wonderful from the maintainer's point of view. However I think there is a cost to the user, at least to users like me. Various autocompleters (especially IPython) and linters use the explicit kwarg declarations to provide a great convenience. And IPython's instant documentation is less useful when the docstring is incomplete. This is one maddening (yet forgivable! Sorry @tacaswell ) thing about matplotlib.

What are your opinions about this? I am willing to do the work of re-adding all the declarations and/or docstrings for the v0.4 release. I don't think that maintaining that state in the v0.2 and v0.3 series was particularly onerous, but it was hardly automatic and it's worth discussing.

issue_title: Request feat(controller): add disk i/o limits
issue_body:
Currently is possible to do something like this:

```
docker run -it --rm --name block-device-test gliderlabs/alpine:3.1 /bin/sh

wget http://www.iozone.org/src/current/iozone3_430.tar

apk-install build-base

tar xpvf iozone3_430.tar
cd iozone3_430/src/current
make linux-AMD64

./iozone -l 32 -O -i 0 -i 1 -i 2 -e -+n -r 4K -s 1G
```

Per se this is not wrong but it will consume all the disk i/o available and start a chain of events that it could put the node in a unusable/inaccessible state for the duration of the task.
Currently in docker/libcontainer there is some work to add support to limit the i/o but is not yet available (using the native driver) as a docker flag:

https://github.com/docker/libcontainer/pull/354
https://github.com/docker/libcontainer/pull/337

Log files stored in /var/lib/deis/store/logs are not limited in size. I've recently had a problem with Ceph deadlock and while investigating, noticed that one of my log files was 18GB large. Looks like there are two improvements that can be made here:

1. There should be configuration option for logger to specify maximum number of log entries (or maximum file size), just like Heroku's 1500 lines limit. This is to avoid huge files on disk. Another option here is some kind of logrotate.

2. There should be configuration option or hardcoded value for maximum logs rate from an app, bytes/second. This is to avoid deadlocks in Ceph MDS when application with multiple containers on several hosts generates high amount of logs.

Please let me know if it makes sense.

issue_title: Required options should become positional arguments
issue_body:
e.g. `globus transfer bookmark show --bookmark-id=<UUID>`
should become `globus transfer bookmark show <UUID>`.

Requires #73 for commands that deal with endpoints and paths to simplify the interface of some commands (e.g. `globus transfer async-transfer`, which without any simplification would require four positional arguments instead of two).

Here is my proposal for how we should add Transfer's upcoming support of symlinks to the SDK:

**Task submission:**

Add `recursive_symlink="ignore"` to `TransferData.__init__`'s named arguments, then add it as one of the `TransferData`'s query paramaters.

SDK will not enforce that the value is in "ignore", "keep", or "copy" to stay in our model of giving back the errors Transfer gives us.

Add `add_symlink_item(self, source_path, destination_path)` to `transferData`'s methods, akin to `TransferDat.add_item` that adds a `transfer_symlink_item` to the DATA field of the submission data.

(could also add `symlink=False` to the existing `add_item` method, but a seperate method avoids the issue of `recursive` and `symlink` both being True.)

No code changes to `DeleteData` `TransferClient.submit_transfer` or `TransferClientsubmit_delete` should be needed.

**Task Monitoring:**

No code changes should be needed for task monitoring as we just return a `TransferResponse` that will now happen to have a few more data fields.

**Symink Creation:**

add an `operation_symlink(self, endpoint_id, symlink_target, path, **params)` method to `TransferClient`.

That makes a POST request to /operation/endpoint/<endpoint_id>/symlink with the given request parameters and returns a `TransferResponse`.

issue_title: netCDF4 can't be used on ssl requests
issue_body:
netCDF4 1.2.2 needs curl 7.45.0 by one of its dependencies 

Unfortunately this version has not anymore SSLv2_client_method defined.

Is there a newer netCDF4 module which did not try to request those old SSLv2 client method or a newer curl which can be replace this 7.45.0 haveing those symbols

issue_title: Explain/improve relocation support for non-relocatable code
issue_body:
I watched the video at http://conda.pydata.org/docs/ and I noticed the claim that Conda does various things about relocating code, but other than mentioning shebangs, not much is said.  Conda seems to depend strongly on all pkgs being relocatable, so this is an important subject.

Because of things like autoconf, a great number of open source codebases are non-relocatable out of the box.  Absolute install paths can be encoded in a large number of places:

 - ELF objects, in rpath/runpath
 - shebangs
 - in object code (for example, valgrind execs a helper program using the absolute install path)
 - in data files

Fixing all of these is hard, as the number of ways in which an absolute install path could be hardcoded at build-time is effectively infinite.

The rpath/runpath case can be addressed with `patchelf(1)` using `$ORIGIN`-relative paths.  Shebangs can be addressed with `sed(1)`.  The rest looks harder to me.  `LD_PRELOAD` can be used to some degree (but not on all platforms) to intercept references to build-time hardcoded install paths and rewrite them to run-time relative install paths.

So I'm curious as to what all Conda does to address this.  Perhaps I've missed something.  (I looked a bit in the sources, but nothing stuck out.)

(It really is a terrible thing that autoconf makes it so easy to write non-relocatable code...)

`conda remove -y _license qt pyqt spyder spyder-app tk ipython-notebook ipython-qtconsole`

Why is it uninstalling all my r dependencies?

When using Python 3.x -based conda environments I am experiencing problems with wrapping of long commands in the IPython terminal:

![screenshot_2016-06-21_15-58-08](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2922144/16234306/fba8e056-37c8-11e6-8945-e6786693c6f1.png)

In this case I was trying to scroll back left along a previous command in order to edit it. The act of scrolling along the line triggered this observed behaviour where the line replicated several times vertically, garbling the line contents as it did so. As evidenced in the outputs I had to Ctrl-C to stop the vertical scrolling.

This happens with every command that I try to edit which exceeds the width of the window.

This behaviour does not occur when I am using my system's IPython with Python 2.7 (i.e. no conda environment active). 

I have previously tried opening a new terminal with a 'clean' .bashrc and adding conda to the PATH myself before activating the environment and this also causes problems so I don't think it's a PS1-related problem, at least not one that I've brought upon myself with a custom .bashrc.

issue_title: Installing numpy and scikit-learn together downgrades to python2
issue_body:
This is for conda version 4.3.30 x86_64.

After conda is installed, the python binary points at `~/miniconda3/bin/python`. The python version is 3.6.3.

I can create an environment without specifying the python version, e.g. `conda create --name my-env`.  Once the environment is created and activated, the python binary still points at `~/miniconda3/bin/python` and it is python 3.6.3.

Now, if I install numpy and scikit-learn like so `conda install numpy scikit-learn`, it will downgrade to python 2.7.  However, installing the 2 packages separately does not downgrade python.

This behavior does not seem to be consistent with the conda documentation.

issue_title: curator v3.2.0 reporting successful indices deletion, but does not actually deleted them
issue_body:
I have a cluster with bunch of old indices that I want to prune, keeping only latest 7 days.  I run the command.

$ curator --port 9217 --timeout 900 delete indices --timestring "%Y.%m.%d" --older-than 7 --time-unit days
2015-06-25 16:54:49,038 INFO      Job starting: delete indices
2015-06-25 16:54:49,628 INFO      Pruning Kibana-related indices to prevent accidental deletion.
2015-06-25 16:54:49,628 WARNING   Very large list of indices.  Breaking it up into smaller chunks.
2015-06-25 16:54:49,629 INFO      Deleting indices as a batch operation:
.....bunch of outputs
2015-06-25 16:55:33,241 INFO      Job completed successfully.

But that is not true.   I checked and there are still many indices that were supposed to be deleted and were not.   So if I run the same command multiple times, eventually all the indices get deleted.

The output of multiple runs is fairly long, I'll put them in a gist here:
https://gist.github.com/TinLe/c64dbe495e0c2d228a6b

The last few runs correctly gave Error or Warnings, so that part is correct.   The error/warning were because the cluster just started creating new daily indices, so were busy with initializing shards (yellow status).   Once the cluster got done with the new indices, the deletion completed fine.   When I started the deletion, the cluster was relatitively quiet and in green status.

The bug is that curator is report "Job completed successfully" when obviously that is not the case.

If I'm trying to reindex some indices to an alias like this:

```
---
actions:
```

per-week-test is an alias for index test-weekly-000001 which gets filled during the action. It seems like the verification after the reindex-operation doesn't work with aliases.

issue_title: Network NIC creation with subnet in different resource group fails
issue_body:
Situation:

Resource group `Production` contains the virtual network `vlan`. In this vnet the subnet `implementation-subnet` is created under the resource group `Production`. In a different resource group `implementation`, a new nic is created with a reference to the subnet `implementation-subnet`, as this resource requires access to the main network. 

In the Azure Portal it is possible to create the above situation. 

Using the Azure-CLI this command fails with `The Resource 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vlan' under resource group 'implementation' was not found.`

```bash
az group create -n Production -l westeurope
az group create -n implementation -l westeurope

az network vnet create -g Production --vnet-name vlan 

az network vnet subnet create -g Production --vnet-name vlan --address-prefix 10.0.100.0/24 --name implementation-subnet

az network nic create --resource-group implementation --name implementation-nic \
  --vnet-name vlan --subnet implementation-subnet \
  --private-ip-address 10.0.100.1
```

Any suggestions how to make this work using the CLI. A workaround is using the Portal, but is not preffered.

issue_title: `az ad sp create-for-rbac` fails even after deleting the created sp
issue_body:
### Description
It seems like `az ad sp create-for-rbac` is creating a service principal only, so after running `az ad sp delete`, deleting the sp leaves the application (which makes sense).

The problem is when deleting a sp created with create-for-rbac, then trying to run `az ad sp create-for-rbac`, the command fails unless you delete the app.

Seems this command should be `az ad app create-for-rbac` or take into account the app may still exist and simply add another sp to the existing app.

```python
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

I've also tried `client.describe_table`, which results in the same error. It seems like the semantics for `StreamReader` have changed such that it can no longer be treated like data.

The crash does not occur with aiohttp 3.0.9.

Environment:
* Python 3.6.4
* aiobotocore 0.6.0
* aiohttp 3.1.0
* botocore 1.8.21

issue_title: Make Meetup Outreach demo more parallelizable (remove use of globals)
issue_body:
The Meetup Outreach demo (see issue #30) makes extensive use of globals in the Python code. While this works well when running the demo locally, it doesn't allow us to make good use of distributed nodes.

We need to replace the use of globals through the use of map/reduce and broadcast vars & accumulators.

[root@]# python -V
Python 2.6.6
[root@]# gixy /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/gixy", line 7, in <module>
    from gixy.cli.main import main
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/gixy/cli/main.py", line 8, in <module>
    from gixy.formatters import get_all as formatters
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/gixy/formatters/__init__.py", line 2, in <module>
    from gixy.formatters.base import BaseFormatter
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/gixy/formatters/base.py", line 7
    skip_parents = {block.Root, block.HttpBlock}
                              ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

Hi there,

First of all, thanks for the work in autogrid, it's amazing to use!

Nonetheless, I am now trying to work with a piecewise linear function with the following form for a single float input

```
def ste(x, phi):
    if x < -phi:
        return -1.
    elif x <= phi:
        return 1. / phi * x
    else:
        return 1.
```

I could vectorise it using numpy indexing in the following way

```
def vvste(x, phi):
    gx = np.copy(x)
    gx[x < -phi] = -1.
    gx[np.abs(x) <= phi] = 1. / phi * gx[np.abs(x) <= phi]
    gx[x > phi] = 1.
    return gx.astype(np.float32)
```

Since this is not supported in autogrid I had to come up with the following implementation

```
def vvste(x, phi):
    condlist = [x < -phi, np.abs(x) < phi, x > phi]
    choicelist = [-1., 1. / phi * x, 1.]
    return np.array(np.select(condlist, choicelist), dtype=np.float32)
```

that looks nice and works but whose derivative is painfully (REALLY) slow. Is there any solution to this?

Thank you!
Alex

Initial setup
-------------

I have a `.coveragerc` file like:

```
[report]
exclude_lines =
    def __repr__
```

I run my tests with `tox` which in turn runs `pytest` with `pytest-cov`, which means my `tox.ini` file looks like this:

```
[testenv]
deps =
    pytest
    pytest-cov
commands = pytest --cov
```

Then:

- When running `tox` in my machine the showed report  correctly excludes those lines.
- When the CI service runs `tox` first, it also shows the correct report, with those lines excluded.
- Just after running `tox`, the CI service runs `codecov` and the report uploaded to Codecov is also correct.


Broken setup
---------------

Now I remove `.coveragerc` file and move those lines to `tox.ini`:

```
[coverage:report]
exclude_lines =
    def __repr__
```

Then:

- When running `tox` in my machine the showed report  correctly excludes those lines.
- When the CI service runs `tox` first, it also shows the correct report, with those lines excluded.
- Just after running `tox`, the CI service runs `codecov` and the report uploaded to Codecov, this time, differs. It does not have those excluded lines, so they are reported as not covered.

Is there any way I could fix that? Is there an option in `codecov` to specify the coverage configuration is in `tox.ini`. Should not `codecov` read that file by default?

This is a request for a higher-level "Python SDK Capabilities" document that articulates the various capabilities enabled by the Python SDK. The existing documentation is excellent; however, a user currently needs to scan through the docs and modules to get an understanding of all the cool things you can do with these libraries.

issue_title: Question: How are you getting detailed error messages?
issue_body:
**Question**:

How is the python client getting such detailed error messages to deliver to the user?

Is it the error that's coming back in the http response, or is it extrapolated from a code number?

I just figured it would be easier to ask some people that know and can answer quickly rather than grep around the code for hours myself.

**Background**:

I had a misconfiguration with my DNS server which was prevented validation.

I first tried using Caddy (an LE client written in go, uses lego) which gave me this:

```
2015/12/06 03:51:44 [blog.coolaj86.com] failed to get certificate: The server could not validate our request.
```

I thought that perhaps the Caddy implementation was simply incomplete, so then I tried letsencrypt's own python client and got this:

```
Failed authorization procedure. blog.coolaj86.com (tls-sni-01): urn:acme:error:unknownHost :: The server could not resolve a domain name :: No IPv4 addresses found for blog.coolaj86.com
```

Over at Caddy Matt I filed an issue "ambiguous error message" (https://github.com/mholt/caddy/issues/393) and he says that all they're seeing is an `invalid` in the response from boulder, so they don't want what error message to give back.

Also following up with lego at https://github.com/xenolf/lego/issues/49

issue_title: Visual Recognition API - calling classify twice with the same file object fails
issue_body:
If you open an image in Python as a file object and pass it to `VisualRecognitionV3.classify` multiple times, it fails every time after the first call.

Here is an example:
```
from watson_developer_cloud import VisualRecognitionV3

api_key="your API key"
custom_classifier_id="your custom classifier ID"
latest_version = VisualRecognitionV3.latest_version
vr = VisualRecognitionV3(latest_version, api_key=api_key)

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(1, 4): # i = 1, 2, 3
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file)
	except: print "general classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(4, 7): # i = 4, 5, 6
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file)
	except: print "general classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(1, 4): # i = 1, 2, 3
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file, classifier_ids=[custom_classifier_id])
	except: print "custom classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(4, 7): # i = 4, 5, 6
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file, classifier_ids=[custom_classifier_id])
	except: print "custom classification attempt %d failed" % i
```

This prints the following:
```
general classification attempt 2 failed
general classification attempt 3 failed
general classification attempt 5 failed
general classification attempt 6 failed
custom classification attempt 2 failed
custom classification attempt 3 failed
custom classification attempt 5 failed
custom classification attempt 6 failed
```

Shouldn't it be possible to pass the same file in?

Thanks!
![screenshot_20160225-124217](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10147901/13335716/3fce612e-dbc7-11e5-8502-d0f7b2869ac7.png)

Marshmallow (at least 3.0.0b8) has an implementation of `Schema.dump` that contains a dict of serialized data. However, flask-marshmallow's `Schema.jsonify` tries to take its `data` attribute. (See [source](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/flask-marshmallow/blob/7208b900ecdac881d272378c4ef1482741fe7b9b/flask_marshmallow/schema.py#L35).)

This seems to be a bug? It least in my code I get this exception:
```
[...]
  File "/[...]/flask_marshmallow/schema.py", line 35, in jsonify
    data = self.dump(obj, many=many).data
AttributeError: 'dict' object has no attribute 'data'
```

issue_title: Strange behaviour when attempting to evaluate batches of sequences
issue_body:
I have the following model defined in keras which I convert to CoreML:

```
SEQUENCE_LENGTH = 8000
NUM_CATS = 1025

inputs = Input(shape=(SEQUENCE_LENGTH,))
L0 = Reshape((SEQUENCE_LENGTH,1))(inputs)
L1 = Conv1D(filters=4,
             kernel_size=4,
             strides=4, 
             padding='same', 
             activation='relu')(L0)
L2  = Dense(32, activation='relu')(L1)
L3 = Dense(NUM_CATS, activation='softmax')(L2)

model = Model(inputs=inputs, outputs=L3)
```

If I run a prediction with one sequence it appears to work correctly:

```
inputx = np.array(X[0])
print(inputx.shape)
t0 = time.time()
print("CoreML model output shape: ", coreml_model.predict({'input1': inputx})['output1'].shape)
print("Time taken: ", time.time()-t0)

(8000,)
CoreML model output shape:  (2000, 1025)
Time taken:  0.049160003662109375
```

However if I run it on multiple sequences the output shape is incorrect:

```
inputx = np.array([X[0:20]])
print(inputx.shape)
t0 = time.time()
print("CoreML model output shape: ", coreml_model.predict({'input1': inputx})['output1'].shape)
print("Time taken: ", time.time()-t0)

(1, 20, 8000)
CoreML model output shape:  (2000, 1025)
Time taken:  0.5843930244445801
```

The output shape should be `(20, 2000, 1025)` not `(2000, 1025)`. The time taken to process has increased by 20x so something is happening. As things stand without the ability to process in batches evaluating this particular model on CPU is faster than GPU.
