hi,

I'm trying to use python 3.5 to run the example channel overriding script:
https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/examples/channel_overrides/channel_overrides.py

But it gave me an error of in the dronekit \__init\__ func:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "channel_overrides.py", line 66, in <module>
    vehicle.channels.overrides['2'] = 200
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/dronekit/\__init\__.py", line 741, in \__setitem\__
    self._send()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/dronekit/\__init\__.py", line 753, in _send
    for k, v in self.iteritems():
AttributeError: 'ChannelsOverride' object has no attribute 'iteritems'

Does anyone know the way to fix the issue? Thanks

We'd like to be able to write code that can verify that a message was received by the autopilot/is being acted on by the autopilot. There are three ways this might be done:
1. Monitor the command ack returned by the message
2. Read back the target setting to confirm it is the same as set (e.g. what is our target speed)
3. Read back the actual change in behaviour (e.g. did our speed actually change)

The second is "best" as it indicates not just that message was received but that is has been stored and will be used. Unfortunately not all targets set can be read (e.g. there is no way to read the target speed currently). 

The third is great in some cases (it is effectively the same as "2") but not in others. For example, reading speed changes to detect if the command to change speed is a non-robust approach if the change value is relatively small.

Approach one is the most "generally" useful approach - reading the command_ack. The downside is that you can associate an ack with a type of command, but not with a particular instance of that command. 

Anyway, there is no proper support for this use case in MAVLink, but it may be possible to use the component ID to provide this ID. The purpose of this issue is to verify how that might work. 

MOre information here: http://discuss.dronecode.org/t/mavlink-2-0-support-command-acknowledgement-identifiers/62

issue_title: Add support for stringable and context to docannotate
issue_body:
There needs to be a way to indicate in the `docannotate` documentation that a function return value should be either shown a string (in which case no type conversion is necessary) or that it is a context.

The proposed syntax is:

```
Returns:
```

issue_title: Improve code coverage of unit tests
issue_body:
Over time our code coverage has slowly declined from a high watermark of 97% to a current value around 87%.  This has primarily been do to new features getting added which have incomplete code coverage.

We should all make an effort to increase code coverage for the 0.9.1 release.  This will likely be a tricky and time consuming effort because a lot of the uncovered code is particularly difficult to unit test.

But on the positive side, our unit tests now run much, much more quickly after the migration from ``pyparsing`` to ``shlex``.  So adding more unit/integration tests should be fine from a performance perspective.

issue_title: Rename all backends to "EmailBackend" for consistency with Django
issue_body:
Django's [built-in email backend](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/email/#email-backends) class names are all `EmailBackend`; the type of backend is indicated by the module:

* django.core.mail.backends.**smtp**.EmailBackend
* django.core.mail.backends.**console**.EmailBackend
* django.core.mail.backends.**filebased**.EmailBackend
* django.core.mail.backends.**locmem**.EmailBackend
* django.core.mail.backends.**dummy**.EmailBackend

Before 1.0-alpha, Anymail should change to follow Django's naming convention:

* anymail.backends.**mailgun.MailgunBackend** → anymail.backends.**mailgun**.EmailBackend
* anymail.backends.**mandrill. MandrillBackend** → anymail.backends.**mandrill**.EmailBackend
* anymail.backends.**postmark.PostmarkBackend** → anymail.backends.**postmark**.EmailBackend
* anymail.backends.**sendgrid.SendGridBackend** → anymail.backends.**sendgrid**.EmailBackend
* anymail.backends.**sparkpost.SparkPostBackend** → anymail.backends.**sparkpost**.EmailBackend

To avoid breaking code, the current names should still be supported with a deprecation warning.

(As an additional benefit, this would eliminate the need for admonishments throughout the docs on how to properly capitalize each ESP's name.)

issue_title: Std::array square bracket assignment not working
issue_body:
Possibly the root of my issue is the lack of apparent documentation on array/pointer binding. If there is such a thing, I would be grateful to be pointed to it.

We have an older code base (and older developers) and have a lot of code using fixed buffers and pointers for dynamic memory.

It's hard to provide a complete working example here, but essentially we have

class SomeClass; // Doesn't much matter what is in this class, I don't think

class AnotherClass {
SomeClass* arrayOfPointers[100];
}

and in python
x=SomeClass()
y=AnotherClass()
y.arrayOfPointers[0]=x


In short, I wanted to instantiate SomeClass in python, then set one of the pointers to that instance so that it is accessible to the class.

The only mention of arrays I could find mentioned using std::array was supported, so I converted to that to avoid the headache of setter/getters (though, with a lot of old code to support it would be nice to know if there was a way to expose a regular pointer/array to python and make it compatible with square bracket notation/__setitem__/__getitem__ but per member variable, not for the class).

Anyways, that seemed to compile and run and y.arrayOfPointers[0] returns "None" as expected. Trying to assign it (y.arrayOfPointers[0]=x), however, does not seem to work. No error messages, but when I try to read after writing, it continues to show None. I could create a new list, set list[0] to x, and then assign the new list to arrayOfPointers as a whole, though, and then it seemed to report back correctly. 

z=[None] * 100
z[0] = x
y.arrayOfPointers = z




In short:
1) Is there something special I need to do to get std::array working correctly for single-member assignment with square brackets, or is this a true bug?

2) Is there a way to expose a c-style fixed array (e.g., not a std::array<>) without ugly code/getters/setters that is compatible with square brackets?

3) Same question as 2, but for a regular pointer used for an old-school dynamically allocated array that would need bounds populated by code?

4) Can someone throw a section about array binding into the docs? Seems like a very common use case and the breadcrumbs are hard to follow. I could only find a couple examples in one of the test scripts which used std::array. I see a lot of mention of NumPy and I'm not sure if that applies for what I need, generic lists of objects/object pointers, I thought that was particularly for number-crunching heavy programs.

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Apache2 Auto-install issues with WSGI server modules...
issue_labels: [{'id': 258836903, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20apache', 'name': 'area: apache', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/Good%20Volunteer%20Task', 'name': 'Good Volunteer Task', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954790, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}, {'id': 483030079, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/soccer%20ball', 'name': 'soccer ball', 'color': '303030', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I attempted to use the letsencrypt-auto script to install a cert on one of my domains that is using WSGI modules on Apache2.  The problem is that when it copies the base .conf file to generate one for the SSL equivalent, it doesn't generate new WSGIDaemonProcess names.

The error you get is as follows:

> Error while running apache2ctl configtest.
> Action 'configtest' failed.
> The Apache error log may have more information.
> AH00526: Syntax error on line 36 of 
> /etc/apache2/sites-enabled/mysite.conf:
> Name duplicates previous WSGI daemon definition.

The Apache log file doesn't have any additional details, but the problem is the following line in the conf files:

```
        WSGIDaemonProcess mydaemon \
            processes=2 \
            threads=1 \
            display-name=%{GROUP} \
            python-path=/var/www/myserver/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages \
            python-eggs=/var/www/myserver/data/python-egg-cache

        WSGIProcessGroup mydaemon
```

The WSGI Daemon Process name must be unique and there's already one in the base mysite.conf file and so the mysite.conf-le-ssl.conf (or whatever it gets called) causes a duplicate name.

I don't know that there's an easy fix for the auto script because I believe it's going to necessitate having two different daemons running -- one for the SSL version and one for unencrypted.  If the user is switching to encrypted only where there's only one daemon running, perhaps it would work, if the unencrypted gets dropped or delegated to the encrypted.

Otherwise, where it will get tricky is whether or not two daemons will play nicely when run from the same setup (i.e. same files).  Now in my case, as evidenced above, it's a Python script and it happens to interface with a mysql engine on the backend, etc.  The mysql daemon should sort out database accesses, but it may be desired (or even necessary) to have separate caches and scripts, etc, between the two for the configuration files and things that would otherwise be co-accessed, as the daemon may not be expecting to have to do file locking and such.

I had a similar issue when setting up my development environment on this server, though that is a completely separate code base with different paths, etc, so I just named it something like "mydaemon-dev" and ran different daemons.  But letsencrypt-auto probably won't know if two daemons can run simultaneously with the same configuration or not, and so it probably can't just change the name to mydaemon-ssl or something for the copy and expect it to work.

The simple solution is for the user to manually add the SSL setup to the server, but what this bug report is about is for adding a detection mechanism to the letencrypt-auto script to see if a WSGIDaemonProcess is defined and properly notify the user that it has to be manually enabled rather than spewing out a bunch of warnings and exiting in a somewhat incomplete state (as I don't think it finished setting up my other virtual domains that otherwise had no issues until I ran it again and excluded this domain).

Thanks!
Donna

With the way this currently works, `_cleanup_challenges` would be called just outside the end of the `with` statement for the case when an exception/signal doesn't occur, but this could/should probably be refactored to have this handled by the `with` statement.

With torchtext v 0.2.1, It seems that the targets returned by the WikiText2 dataset correspond to the next window of `bptt_len` over the dataset. For a language modeling task, shouldn't the targets be simply the next word? 

concretely: `batch.text =  ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']` and `batch.target =  ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']` and it is my understanding that `batch.target = ['=']` would be correct.


full testing code:
```python

from torchtext import data
from torchtext import datasets

TEXT = data.Field()

train_data, val_data, test_data = datasets.WikiText2.splits(TEXT)

TEXT.build_vocab(train_data, min_freq=10)

train_iter, val_iter, test_iter = data.BPTTIterator.splits(
            (train_data, val_data, test_data),
            batch_size=1, bptt_len=5, repeat=False)

batch = next(iter(train_iter))

def recover_text(vector):
    return [TEXT.vocab.itos[int(v)] for v in vector]

recover_text(batch.text.data.squeeze())  # >>> ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']

recover_text(batch.target.data.squeeze()) # >>> ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']
```

issue_title: ComponentFailureTest.testPlasmaManagerFailed test hangs frequently in Travis in MacOS/Python3 build.
issue_body:
The following test fails nearly every time in Travis on the MacOS/Python 3.5 build.

```
python test/component_failures_test.py ComponentFailureTest.testPlasmaManagerFailed
```

Note that the line https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/617bc4d2394aec607755a1a33f16271c38298f3f/test/component_failures_test.py#L134 has a bug, it should just be `num_workers_per_scheduler`. After making that change, I can reproduce the problem locally by running the test in a loop.

Investigating the bug a bit more, it appears that **somehow a task is assigned to a local scheduler that has been removed**. This shouldn't happen because the monitor should iterate through all of the tasks in the task table and clean them up. However, there may be a race condition.

import ray
from ray.tune.registry import register_env
from ray.rllib import ppo


def create_my_env():
    import gym
    return gym.make("CartPole-v1")

env_creator = lambda :create_my_env()
env_creator_name = "myenv"
register_env(env_creator_name, env_creator)
ray.init()

for i in range(10):
    result = alg.train()
    print(result)

### Describe the problem
myenv does not exist. the example in the document of ray does not work
http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rllib.html#custom-environments

this can be fix by following
alg = ppo.PPOAgent(env=env_creator_name, registry=ray.tune.registry._default_registry)

however, i think this is not very elegant to use in this way.

I think abstract definitions of the environment in ray rllib should be explicitly written into the document to make it clear for users to customize their environment.

for example:

```
class env(object):
    """
    observation space
    action space
    """
    def step(self, action):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def reset(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def render(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def close(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    """
    ...
    """
```

I think in the application of reinforcement learning in practice, the form of the environment often exists as a remote service address.

```
class env(object):
    """
    observation space
    action space
    """
    def step(self, action):
        import requests
        return requests.post(url, json=params)
    
    def reset(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def render(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def close(self):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    """
    ...
    """
```

Batch creation environment may need to take parameters

```
class BatchedEnv(object):
    """This holds multiple gym envs and performs steps on all of them."""
    def __init__(self, env_creator, batchsize, options):
        self.envs = [env_creator() for _ in range(batchsize)]
```


Thank you for your excellent work

issue_title: Opset version in backends that support Onnx importing
issue_body: Hello! I am trying to survey which backend/s to enable onnx importing. I've installed caffe2 and am able to import an onnx model, but I've realized that the installed caffe2 backend only supports models that were generated using opset 6. How can I easily find which opset a backend supports?

The [IR spec](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/IR.md) seems to use float16/float32/float64 (:+1:) while the [code](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/defs/schema.h#L325-L326) and [op schemas](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md) use float/double (:-1:). Should this be uniform?

issue_title: pad values of convolution don't seems to be correct
issue_body:
Hi,
I am facing a problem with model from onnx/models at https://github.com/onnx/models 

The "conv" nodes have "pads" attribute which represents zero values to add before or after each spacial dimension.

So, normally, the output shape should be : 
out_width = (in_width + pad_left + pad_right - kernel_width) / stride_width + 1

The main utility the have the pad_right pad_left is to be sure to have 
(in_width + pad_left + pad_right - kernel_w) % stride == 0.
This gives the following property:
in_width + pad_left + pad_right = (out_width - 1)*stride_width  +  kernel_width
That you can logically verify when apply a convolution with explicit padding value before and after.

I think if onnx wants to be a truly open exchange format, this rule should be guaranty. 

But when I take a look to the model zoo of onnx, I can see that this rule is not respected.

Examples : 
**inception_v1** first convolution:
pad_left = 3
pad_left = 3
kernel_w = 7
stride_width = 2
in_width = 224
-> (in_width + pad_left + pad_right - kernel_w) % stride != 0

**Squeezenet** first convolution:
pad_left = 0
pad_left = 0
kernel_w = 3
stride_width = 2
in_width = 224
-> (in_width + pad_left + pad_right - kernel_w) % stride != 0

Can someone explain to me the onnx position about padding value ? 
And why this occur ?
For me, it is a bug in the front-end which produced the onnx models.

issue_title: Add an option to return the geojson as a dict instead of writting in a file
issue_body:
When the output folder is not specified it would make sense to consider that the user wants the geojson returned as a `dict`. It will let the user the possibility to modify the geojson before dumping it. At the moment one has to do 2 I/O to do so. 

I can PR if you agree on it

If a rule has a dependency in its condition section which is in parantheses, it will be missed by detect_dependencies().

Example:
```
rule a {
    condition:
    true
}

rule b {
    condition:
    (a) and true
}
```

detect_dependendies() will not detect that rule b references rule a. Remove the parantheses around a in b's condition section and it works.
Some open source yara repositories have rules with dependencies in parantheses.

Using HEAD version of simplejson:

    In [3]: simplejson.dumps([0.3])
    Out[3]: '[0.29999999999999999]'

I would expect this to output `'[0.3]'`. A workaround is to manually turn the fractions into strings before encoding.
